tweets.text:
Saylor just admitted to completely shifting the focus for Strategy.

He defended the weekend raise on a total assets per share basis instead of BTC Yield:

“when both assets are included, it was accretive”

That’s a huge departure after years of preaching BPS (Bitcoin per share) as the metric that matters.

MSTR’s premium (now trading ~1.35x NAV - the Bitcoin minus the ~$22.7B of debt and preferred ahead of it) has never been about a screenshot of the static balance sheet.

The market pays for future BPS growth and leverage.

The moment the standard becomes “current net assets per share,” a static pile should trade near 1x.

So he’s using NAV math to win this argument while the entire valuation rests on the BPS growth frame he’s stepping away from.

NAV defends the deal but condemns the premium.

BPS growth defends the premium but condemns the deal.

He can’t keep both.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: @mattkratter BTC Yield measures the increase in BTC per share, not total shareholder accretion. Last week Strategy added ₿1,550 of BTC and $100 million of USD Reserve. When both assets are included, the transaction was accretive to MSTR shareholders.

tweets.text:
Read this if you're wondering why I'm so bearish on Saylor and his Strategy / $STRC ponzi.

He got greedy and overleveraged, which has forced him to break foundational promises and make every $MSTR holder pay for it.

I believe in Bitcoin.

But the blatant worship it's become of one man and one company by "Bitcoin Maximalists" is absurd.

The way so many people try and claim that it's success is dependent on this man pressing the buy button over and over again is also absurd.

Flush the leverage and the idolatry, and then proceed sending everything to new all time highs with haste.

thread_tweets.text:
I'll be the first to admit how much I wanted it to work and refused to think critically about some of the cracks that could happen, but when Saylor started breaking promises and moving the goal posts it forced me to be more objective.

So I spent weeks doing research to find any way possible to try and justify ways he could continue to prop this mess up, but the more time I spent digging the more I realized just how bad everything possibly *could* get... and the article walks through those findings.

tweets.text: @mattkratter BTC Yield measures the increase in BTC per share, not total shareholder accretion. Last week Strategy added ₿1,550 of BTC and $100 million of USD Reserve. When both assets are included, the transaction was accretive to MSTR shareholders.

thread_tweets.text: @mattkratter BTC Yield measures the increase in BTC per share, not total shareholder accretion. Last week Strategy added ₿1,550 of BTC and $100 million of USD Reserve. When both assets are included, the transaction was accretive to MSTR shareholders.
thread_tweets.full_text: @mattkratter BTC Yield measures the increase in BTC per share, not total shareholder accretion. Last week Strategy added ₿1,550 of BTC and $100 million of USD Reserve. When both assets are included, the transaction was accretive to MSTR shareholders.

thread_tweets.text: The document you cite clearly states that BTC Yield is a narrow KPI used to assess per-share accretion solely as it pertains to bitcoin holdings. It is not a measure of financial performance, valuation, liquidity, ROI, book value, or stockholder return. Full financial analysis requires a more comprehensive review of our financial statements and SEC disclosures.
thread_tweets.full_text: The document you cite clearly states that BTC Yield is a narrow KPI used to assess per-share accretion solely as it pertains to bitcoin holdings. It is not a measure of financial performance, valuation, liquidity, ROI, book value, or stockholder return. Full financial analysis requires a more comprehensive review of our financial statements and SEC disclosures.

tweets.full_text:
@mattkratter BTC Yield measures the increase in BTC per share, not total shareholder accretion. Last week Strategy added ₿1,550 of BTC and $100 million of USD Reserve. When both assets are included, the transaction was accretive to MSTR shareholders.

The document you cite clearly states that BTC Yield is a narrow KPI used to assess per-share accretion solely as it pertains to bitcoin holdings. It is not a measure of financial performance, valuation, liquidity, ROI, book value, or stockholder return. Full financial analysis requires a more comprehensive review of our financial statements and SEC disclosures.
